edges.description: Dysregulated calcium leads to impaired Purkinje cell pacemaking and downstream excitotoxic stress, ultimately causing cell death.
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Reduced EAAT4 surface expression causes glutamate accumulation and excitotoxic stress in Purkinje cells.
edges.source: Cytoskeletal and Scaffold Protein Defects in Purkinje Cells
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: Tau-Tubulin Kinase 2 Loss and Purkinje Cell Dysfunction
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy
Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: IP3R1 Haploinsufficiency and Calcium Release Defects
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Purkinje-cell degeneration disrupts cerebellar gait and stance control, producing progressive gait ataxia.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Loss of cerebellar cortical output impairs limb coordination, producing limb ataxia.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Cerebellar cortical degeneration impairs speech coordination, causing dysarthria.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: Cerebellar oculomotor network degeneration causes downbeat or positioning nystagmus.
edges.source: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: Pentanucleotide Repeat RNA Foci in SCA31
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: Neprilysin-Dependent Neuropeptide Catabolism Defect in SCA43
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: Candidate PLD3-Linked Lysosomal and Synaptic Mechanism in SCA46
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.source: Mitochondrial dsRNA Accumulation and Interferon Activation in SCA25
edges.target: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

edges.description: PNPT1-linked mitochondrial dsRNA stress is proposed to contribute to the sensory ganglionopathy that makes SCA25 an exception to the otherwise pure-cerebellar ADCA-III pattern.
intermediate_mechanisms: sensory ganglionopathy and peripheral axonal involvement in SCA25
edges.source: Mitochondrial dsRNA Accumulation and Interferon Activation in SCA25

nodes.description: Exome sequencing and network analysis associated PLD3 with SCA46 and linked genetically diverse spinocerebellar ataxias to synaptic transmission and related cellular pathways. PLD3 is retained as candidate or partial evidence because later work has questioned the strength of PLD3 causality.
nodes.id: Candidate PLD3-Linked Lysosomal and Synaptic Mechanism in SCA46

evidence.reference_title: Exome sequencing and network analysis identifies shared mechanisms underlying spinocerebellar ataxia.
evidence.snippet: Here, we report five novel spinocerebellar ataxia genes, FAT2, PLD3, KIF26B, EP300, and FAT1

nodes.description: SCA5 is caused by variants in SPTBN2 encoding beta-III spectrin, a major cytoskeletal scaffold protein in Purkinje cells. Beta-III spectrin stabilises the dendritic membrane, anchors the glutamate transporter EAAT4 and other postsynaptic proteins, and supports dendritic arborisation. Loss-of-function or dominant-negative variants reduce EAAT4 surface expression, impairing glutamate clearance at Purkinje cell dendrites and increasing excitotoxic stress.
nodes.id: Cytoskeletal and Scaffold Protein Defects in Purkinje Cells

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 5.
evidence.snippet: mutant β-III spectrin fails to stabilize the glutamate transporter EAAT4 at the plasma membrane. Additionally, marked differences in EAAT4 and GluRδ2 were found by protein blot and cell fractionation in SCA5 autopsy tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Mutant β-III spectrin causes mGluR1α mislocalization and functional deficits in a mouse model of spinocerebellar ataxia type 5.
evidence.snippet: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 5 (SCA5), a dominant neurodegenerative disease characterized by profound Purkinje cell loss, is caused by mutations in SPTBN2, a gene that encodes β-III spectrin.

nodes.description: Cerebellar dysarthria (scanning, poorly coordinated speech) is a common accompaniment as the disorder progresses.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 11.
evidence.snippet: speech and language therapy for dysarthria and dysphagia

nodes.description: SCA15/SCA16 is caused by heterozygous deletions or missense variants in ITPR1, encoding the inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor type 1 (IP3R1), the dominant calcium-release channel on the endoplasmic reticulum of cerebellar Purkinje cells. Haploinsufficiency of ITPR1 impairs IP3-mediated calcium release, disrupting the calcium signals that regulate Purkinje cell excitability, LTD at parallel fibre synapses, and climbing fibre synapse elimination during development.
nodes.id: IP3R1 Haploinsufficiency and Calcium Release Defects

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous deletion of ITPR1, but not SUMF1, in spinocerebellar ataxia type 16.
evidence.snippet: Our data provide evidence that haploinsufficiency of ITPR1 alone causes SCA16 and SCA15.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 15.
evidence.snippet: SCA15 is due to various deletions of the inositol 1,4,5-triphosphate receptor 1 gene (ITPR1) on the distal short arm of chromosome 3.

nodes.description: Cerebellar limb ataxia — dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, and intention tremor — is a consistent feature across ADCA-III subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: A clinical and genetic study in a large cohort of patients with spinocerebellar ataxia type 6.
evidence.snippet: cerebellar signs were detected in nearly 100% of the patients. In contrast, extracerebellar signs were relatively mild and infrequent.

nodes.description: Heterozygous loss-of-function variants in PNPT1 reduce PNPase (polyribonucleotide nucleotidyltransferase 1) activity, impairing surveillance and degradation of mitochondrial double-stranded RNA. Accumulation of dsRNA in mitochondria leads to its leakage into the cytoplasm, triggering a type I interferon response. This innate immune activation is proposed to underlie the sensory ganglionopathy and cerebellar neurodegeneration characteristic of SCA25.
nodes.id: Mitochondrial dsRNA Accumulation and Interferon Activation in SCA25

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous PNPT1 Variants Cause Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 25.
evidence.snippet: PNPase notably prevents the abnormal accumulation of double-stranded mtRNAs in the mitochondria and leakage into the cytoplasm, associated with triggering a type I interferon response.

nodes.description: Dominant MME variants such as p.C143Y implicate neprilysin, a membrane metalloprotease expressed in the central and peripheral nervous systems. Impaired neprilysin-dependent peptide handling may alter dynorphin or other neuropeptide catabolism, contributing to cerebellar dysfunction with peripheral neuropathy in SCA43.
nodes.id: Neprilysin-Dependent Neuropeptide Catabolism Defect in SCA43

evidence.reference_title: MME mutation in dominant spinocerebellar ataxia with neuropathy (SCA43).
evidence.snippet: NEP acts on many substrates, including neuropetides such as glucagon, enkephalins, cholecystokinin, neuropeptide Y, substance P, somatostatin, neurotensin, oxytocin, prodynorphin and bradykinin, as well as the amyloid beta (Aβ) peptides accumulated in Alzheimer disease (AD).

nodes.description: Downbeat or gaze-evoked nystagmus is frequently observed across ADCA-III subtypes, reflecting cerebellar oculomotor control dysfunction. Downbeat positioning nystagmus is particularly characteristic of SCA6.

evidence.reference_title: A clinical and genetic study in a large cohort of patients with spinocerebellar ataxia type 6.
evidence.snippet: There was a close relationship between downbeat positioning nystagmus (DPN) and positioning vertigo, which became more common in the later stage.

nodes.description: SCA14 is caused by missense variants in PRKCG encoding protein kinase C gamma (PKCgamma), which is highly and selectively expressed in Purkinje cells and plays critical roles in long-term depression (LTD) at parallel fibre-Purkinje cell synapses. Pathogenic PRKCG missense variants lead to constitutive activation or aggregation of PKCgamma, disrupting synaptic plasticity. Misfolded mutant PKCgamma can aggregate and trigger Purkinje cell apoptosis.

evidence.reference_title: Congo red, an amyloid-inhibiting compound, alleviates various types of cellular dysfunction triggered by mutant protein kinase cγ that causes spinocerebellar ataxia type 14 (SCA14) by inhibiting oligomerization and aggregation.
evidence.snippet: We previously demonstrated that the mutant γPKC found in SCA14 is susceptible to aggregation that induces apoptotic cell death.

evidence.reference_title: Calcium Signaling, PKC Gamma, IP3R1 and CAR8 Link Spinocerebellar Ataxias and Purkinje Cell Dendritic Development.
evidence.snippet: PKCγ dysregulation causes abnormal Purkinje cell dendritic development and SCA14.

nodes.description: SCA31 is caused by an intronic BEAN1/TK2-region pentanucleotide repeat insertion. Repeat-containing transcripts form RNA foci in Purkinje-cell nuclei and bind serine/arginine-rich splicing factors, supporting an RNA-foci and splicing-factor sequestration mechanism.
nodes.id: Pentanucleotide Repeat RNA Foci in SCA31

biological_processes: regulation of alternative mRNA splicing, via spliceosome

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 31 is associated with "inserted" penta-nucleotide repeats containing (TGGAA)n.
evidence.snippet: The repeat insertion was located in introns of TK2 and BEAN (brain expressed, associated with Nedd4) expressed in the brain and formed RNA foci in the nuclei of patients' Purkinje cells.

nodes.description: Peripheral nerve involvement is absent or minimal in most ADCA-III subtypes, in contrast to many ADCA-I subtypes. Exceptions include SCA25 and SCA43, where sensory ganglionopathy or axonal neuropathy can be prominent, and SCA23/SCA46, where sensory neuropathy has been described in some families.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 11.
evidence.snippet: Peripheral neuropathy and dystonia are rare.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous PNPT1 Variants Cause Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 25.
evidence.snippet: The SCA25 phenotype spans a slowly evolving sensory and cerebellar ataxia, in most cases attributed to ganglionopathy.

evidence.reference_title: MME mutation in dominant spinocerebellar ataxia with neuropathy (SCA43).
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals presented with late-onset sensorimotor axonal polyneuropathy; all but one also had cerebellar ataxia.

nodes.description: PDYN missense variants alter prodynorphin-derived dynorphin peptides, increasing mutant dynorphin A toxicity and disrupting opioid/glutamate signaling. The resulting glutamate neurotoxicity is proposed to drive Purkinje cell degeneration and ataxia in SCA23.

evidence.reference_title: Prodynorphin mutations cause the neurodegenerative disorder spinocerebellar ataxia type 23.
evidence.snippet: alterations in Dyn A activities and/or impairment of secretory pathways by mutant PDYN may lead to glutamate neurotoxicity, which underlies Purkinje cell degeneration and ataxia.

nodes.description: Progressive gait and stance ataxia is the cardinal symptom shared by all ADCA-III subtypes, reflecting loss of Purkinje cell inhibitory output to deep cerebellar nuclei.

evidence.reference_title: A clinical and genetic study in a large cohort of patients with spinocerebellar ataxia type 6.
evidence.snippet: Clinically, unsteadiness of gait was the main initial symptom, followed by vertigo and oscillopsia, and cerebellar signs were detected in nearly 100% of the patients.

evidence.reference_title: Gait impairment precedes clinical symptoms in spinocerebellar ataxia type 6.
evidence.snippet: Discrete gait characteristics precede symptoms in SCA6 with significantly increased variability of step width and step time, whereas a more global gait deficit was evident in symptomatic individuals.

nodes.description: A central convergence point across ADCA-III subtypes is disrupted calcium homeostasis in Purkinje cells. In SCA6, small CAG repeat expansions in CACNA1A produce a toxic polyglutamine domain in the C-terminal cytoplasmic tail of Cav2.1, reducing channel density at Purkinje cell dendrites and impairing calcium-dependent pacemaking. This impairs the finely tuned calcium oscillations required for Purkinje cell pacemaking and synaptic plasticity. The IP3R1 mechanism in SCA15 is described separately in the IP3R1 Haploinsufficiency node.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 6 knockin mice develop a progressive neuronal dysfunction with age-dependent accumulation of mutant CaV2.1 channels.
evidence.snippet: The mice expressing hyperexpanded polyglutamine (Sca6(84Q)) developed progressive motor impairment and aggregation of mutant Ca(V)2.1 channels.

evidence.reference_title: Calcium Signaling, PKC Gamma, IP3R1 and CAR8 Link Spinocerebellar Ataxias and Purkinje Cell Dendritic Development.
evidence.snippet: IP3R1, CAR8 and PKCγ proteins are strongly and specifically expressed in Purkinje cells. The common function among them is that they are involved in the regulation of calcium homeostasis in Purkinje cells and their dysfunction causes ataxia in mouse and human.

nodes.description: Despite diverse upstream molecular defects, all ADCA-III subtypes converge on selective degeneration of cerebellar Purkinje cells. Loss of Purkinje cells impairs the inhibitory output of the cerebellar cortex to the deep cerebellar nuclei, disrupting the cerebellar circuitry that coordinates movement. In ADCA-III, degeneration is restricted to the cerebellar cortex without significant involvement of brainstem, spinal cord, basal ganglia, or peripheral nerves — distinguishing these disorders from ADCA types I and II.
nodes.id: Purkinje Cell Degeneration and Cerebellar Cortical Atrophy

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal dominant cerebellar ataxia type III: a review of the phenotypic and genotypic characteristics.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal Dominant Cerebellar Ataxia (ADCA) Type III is a type of spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA) classically characterized by pure cerebellar ataxia

evidence.reference_title: Gene dosage effect in spinocerebellar ataxia type 6 homozygotes: A clinical and neuropathological study.
evidence.snippet: Neuropathology revealed neuronal loss, as well as α1A-calcium channel protein aggregates in Purkinje cells, a few α1A-calcium channel protein aggregates in the neocortex and basal ganglia, and neuronal loss in Clarke's column and the globus pallidus not seen in heterozygotes.

evidence.reference_title: The wide spectrum of spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs).
evidence.snippet: Three patterns of atrophy can be identified on brain MRI: a pure cerebellar atrophy, a pattern of olivopontocerebellar atrophy, and a pattern of global brain atrophy.

nodes.description: No disease-modifying therapy exists for any ADCA-III subtype. Management centres on multidisciplinary rehabilitation — physiotherapy for balance and gait training, speech therapy for dysarthria and dysphagia, and occupational therapy for daily living adaptations. Assistive devices (canes, walkers, wheelchairs) are introduced as needed.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 11.
evidence.snippet: Management is supportive; there are no known disease-modifying treatments to date.

nodes.description: SCA11 is caused by truncating variants in TTBK2, encoding tau-tubulin kinase 2. TTBK2 is required for ciliogenesis — it phosphorylates cilia-associated proteins to remove the capping complex CP110 from the mother centriole — and it also phosphorylates tau and MARK proteins involved in microtubule stability. Loss of TTBK2 impairs cilia formation and microtubule dynamics, with particular vulnerability in Purkinje cells.
nodes.id: Tau-Tubulin Kinase 2 Loss and Purkinje Cell Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Missense variant in TTBK2 kinase domain causes loss of function and impaired protein phosphorylation.
evidence.snippet: Tau tubulin kinase 2 (TTBK2) is a ubiquitous serine-threonine protein kinase implicated in diverse cellular processes, including microtubule regulation, ciliogenesis, synaptic signaling, and the phosphorylation of key proteins like TDP-43.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 11.
evidence.snippet: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 11 (SCA11) is characterized by progressive cerebellar ataxia and abnormal eye signs (jerky pursuit, horizontal and vertical nystagmus). Pyramidal features are seen on occasion.
